id.description: Unique patient identifier used for tracking records across the study. Not used for prediction modeling.

age.description: Patient age in years at study enrollment. Age is a critical predictor of mortality in critically ill patients, with older patients typically facing higher risk. Range: typically 18-100 years.
age.clinical_significance: Strong predictor of mortality; older age associated with increased risk

death.description: Binary indicator of death at any time up to National Death Index (NDI) data on December 31, 1994. Represents longer-term mortality outcome beyond hospital discharge. Values: 0=alive, 1=died.
death.clinical_significance: Long-term mortality outcome including post-discharge deaths

sex.description: Biological sex of the patient. Categories: male, female. Sex can influence disease presentation, treatment response, and mortality risk in certain conditions.
sex.clinical_significance: May affect disease prognosis and treatment outcomes

hospdead.description: In-hospital mortality indicator. Records whether patient died during hospitalization before discharge. Values: 0=survived to discharge, 1=died in hospital. This is a critical short-term outcome measure.
hospdead.clinical_significance: Primary outcome for acute care prediction models; immediate mortality risk

slos.description: Days from study entry to hospital discharge. Measures length of stay after enrollment. Not used for prediction as it's only known post-outcome. Longer stays may indicate complications or slow recovery.
slos.clinical_significance: Outcome variable; indicates resource utilization but causes data leakage if used as predictor

d.time.description: Total days of follow-up from study entry to last observation or death. Used for survival analysis but not for binary classification prediction due to temporal leakage.
d.time.clinical_significance: Follow-up duration; essential for survival analysis but not for admission-time prediction

dzgroup.description: Detailed disease subcategory classification. Categories: ARF/MOSF with Sepsis (acute respiratory failure/multiple organ system failure with sepsis), CHF (congestive heart failure), COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), Cirrhosis, Colon Cancer, Coma, Lung Cancer, MOSF with Malignancy. Primary diagnosis strongly predicts mortality.
dzgroup.clinical_significance: Primary diagnosis; different disease groups have vastly different mortality rates

dzclass.description: Broader disease category grouping. Categories: ARF/MOSF (acute conditions), COPD/CHF/Cirrhosis (chronic conditions), Cancer, Coma. Provides higher-level disease classification for mortality stratification.
dzclass.clinical_significance: Major disease category; helps stratify baseline mortality risk

num.co.description: Number of comorbidities (simultaneous diseases) present. Higher comorbidity count indicates greater disease burden and complexity, strongly associated with increased mortality risk. Typical range: 0-10+.
num.co.clinical_significance: Higher comorbidity burden strongly predicts worse outcomes and mortality

edu.description: Years of formal education completed. Socioeconomic indicator that may correlate with health literacy, access to care, and baseline health status. Categories typically include ranges like <12, 12, >12 years.
edu.clinical_significance: Socioeconomic proxy; may indicate health literacy and access to preventive care

income.description: Annual household income level. Categories: under $11k, $11-$25k, $25-$50k, >$50k. Socioeconomic status affects access to healthcare, medication adherence, and overall health status.
income.clinical_significance: Economic status may affect care access, medication compliance, and baseline health

scoma.description: Glasgow Coma Scale score on day 3 of ICU admission. Measures level of consciousness (best response in eye opening, verbal, and motor domains). Range: 3 (deep coma) to 15 (fully alert). Lower scores indicate severe neurological impairment and higher mortality risk.
scoma.clinical_significance: Critical neurological indicator; low scores predict high mortality

meanbp.description: Mean arterial blood pressure (MAP) measured on day 3, in mmHg. Calculated as (systolic + 2×diastolic)/3. Normal range: 70-100 mmHg. MAP <65 indicates hypotension/shock requiring intervention. Critical marker of circulatory function and organ perfusion.
meanbp.clinical_significance: Essential perfusion marker; low MAP indicates shock and predicts organ failure

wblc.description: White blood cell count on day 3, measured in thousands per microliter (×10³/μL). Normal: 4-11. Elevated (>11) suggests infection/inflammation; very low (<4) may indicate immunosuppression or bone marrow dysfunction. Both extremes predict poor outcomes.
wblc.clinical_significance: Infection/inflammation marker; abnormal values indicate immune response or dysfunction

hrt.description: Heart rate (pulse) on day 3, in beats per minute (bpm). Normal: 60-100 bpm. Tachycardia (>100) may indicate stress, fever, sepsis, or shock. Bradycardia (<60) may indicate cardiac conduction problems or medication effects.
hrt.clinical_significance: Cardiac function and stress response indicator; abnormal rates predict complications

resp.description: Respiratory rate on day 3, breaths per minute. Normal: 12-20. Tachypnea (>20) indicates respiratory distress, metabolic acidosis, or compensatory response. Critical marker of respiratory and metabolic function.
resp.clinical_significance: Respiratory status marker; elevated rates indicate distress or metabolic compensation

temp.description: Body temperature on day 3, in degrees Celsius. Normal: 36.5-37.5°C. Fever (>38°C) suggests infection. Hypothermia (<36°C) may indicate septic shock or severe illness. Both extremes predict mortality.
temp.clinical_significance: Infection indicator and metabolic status marker; abnormal values predict poor outcomes

pafi.description: PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio on day 3. Ratio of arterial oxygen partial pressure to fraction of inspired oxygen. Normal: >300. <300 indicates acute lung injury; <200 indicates ARDS (acute respiratory distress syndrome). Critical indicator of lung function and oxygenation.
pafi.clinical_significance: Lung oxygenation capacity; low values indicate respiratory failure and high mortality

alb.description: Serum albumin level on day 3, in g/dL. Normal: 3.5-5.0. Low albumin (hypoalbuminemia) indicates poor nutritional status, liver dysfunction, or severe illness. Strong predictor of mortality.
alb.clinical_significance: Nutritional and liver function marker; low levels predict poor outcomes

bili.description: Bilirubin level on day 3, in mg/dL. Normal: <1.2. Elevated bilirubin indicates liver dysfunction, hemolysis, or biliary obstruction. High levels suggest organ failure.
bili.clinical_significance: Liver function indicator; elevated levels indicate hepatic dysfunction

crea.description: Serum creatinine level on day 3, in mg/dL. Normal: 0.6-1.2. Elevated creatinine indicates kidney dysfunction or acute kidney injury. Critical marker of renal function and fluid balance.
crea.clinical_significance: Kidney function marker; elevated levels indicate renal failure risk

sod.description: Serum sodium concentration on day 3, in mEq/L. Normal: 135-145. Hyponatremia (<135) or hypernatremia (>145) indicate electrolyte imbalance affecting cellular function and neurological status.
sod.clinical_significance: Electrolyte balance indicator; abnormalities affect cardiac and neurological function

ph.description: Arterial blood pH on day 3. Normal: 7.35-7.45. Acidosis (<7.35) or alkalosis (>7.45) indicate metabolic or respiratory dysfunction. Critical marker of acid-base balance.
ph.clinical_significance: Acid-base status; abnormal pH indicates metabolic or respiratory failure

glucose.description: Blood glucose level on day 3, in mg/dL. Normal: 70-100 (fasting). Hyperglycemia (>140) common in critically ill; hypoglycemia (<70) dangerous. Both extremes predict complications.
glucose.clinical_significance: Metabolic status; abnormal glucose indicates stress response or metabolic dysfunction

bun.description: Blood urea nitrogen on day 3, in mg/dL. Normal: 7-20. Elevated BUN indicates kidney dysfunction, dehydration, or increased protein catabolism. Marker of renal function and fluid status.
bun.clinical_significance: Renal function and hydration status; elevated levels indicate kidney dysfunction

urine.description: Urine output on day 3, typically in mL per time period. Low urine output (oliguria) indicates kidney dysfunction or inadequate perfusion. Critical marker of renal function and fluid balance.
urine.clinical_significance: Kidney function and perfusion marker; low output indicates acute kidney injury

adlp.description: Activities of Daily Living index (patient-reported) on day 3. Assesses functional independence in basic tasks (bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, feeding). Higher scores indicate better baseline functional status.
adlp.clinical_significance: Baseline functional independence; higher values indicate better pre-illness status

adls.description: Activities of Daily Living index (surrogate/family-reported) on day 3. Alternative assessment when patient cannot self-report. Predicts recovery potential and mortality.
adls.clinical_significance: Baseline function proxy; indicates pre-hospitalization independence level

sfdm2.description: Functional disability level at 2 months post-admission, scored 1-5 (5=highest severity). Based on Sickness Impact Profile (SIP) questionnaires. Alternative outcome measure to mortality.
sfdm2.clinical_significance: Longer-term functional outcome; assesses quality of survival beyond mortality

adlsc.description: Imputed ADL score calibrated to surrogate reports. Standardized version filling gaps where direct patient or surrogate reports unavailable. Used for consistent functional status assessment.
adlsc.clinical_significance: Standardized baseline function measure; predicts recovery trajectory
